title: Biostatistics for Surgeons
subtitle: Academic Surgery — Hypothesis Testing, Regression, Survival Analysis & Bayesian Methods
guidelines_version: CONSORT 2010 Statistical Analysis · STROBE Checklist 2007 · TRIPOD 2015 · GRADE 2011
textbook_refs: Greenhalgh T. How to Read a Paper, 6th ed.
Glantz SA. Primer of Biostatistics, 8th ed.
Altman DG. Practical Statistics for Medical Research. Chapman & Hall, 1991
Harrell FE. Regression Modeling Strategies, 2nd ed. Springer 2015
Gelman A & Hill J. Data Analysis Using Regression and Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge 2007
learning_objectives: Distinguish parametric from non-parametric tests and select the correct test for a given dataset
Interpret hazard ratios, Kaplan-Meier curves, and log-rank tests from surgical survival studies
Calculate and interpret sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and likelihood ratios
Define Type I error (α), Type II error (β), power, and their role in sample size calculation
Identify p-value misinterpretation and explain the role of confidence intervals
Interpret odds ratios from logistic regression and hazard ratios from Cox models
Describe the applications of mixed effects models for repeated measures data
Apply Bayesian concepts to the interpretation of surgical trial evidence
Distinguish ROC curves and AUC as measures of discriminative ability
Identify approaches to missing data including MCAR, MAR, and MNAR

blocks.content: Selecting the correct statistical test requires knowing: (1) the data type (continuous, ordinal, categorical); (2) the number of groups being compared; (3) whether the data are paired or independent; (4) whether the data follow a normal distribution (parametric vs non-parametric decision). Applying the wrong test produces invalid p-values — a frequent source of methodological error in surgical literature. The commonest error is using a t-test on skewed data (e.g., length of stay, blood loss) where a Mann-Whitney U test or log-transformation is required.

blocks.title: Statistical Test Selection Guide
rows: Kaplan-Meier + log-rank test; Cox regression for HRs

blocks.title: Board Pearl — p-value Misconceptions
blocks.content: The p-value is **not** the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is the probability of observing results as extreme as those obtained (or more extreme) if the null hypothesis were true. A p-value of 0.03 does not mean there is a 3% chance the finding is due to chance — that interpretation is incorrect. Always report the effect size and 95% confidence interval alongside the p-value. A small study can produce a clinically important difference that is statistically non-significant (insufficient power); a large study can produce a statistically significant but clinically trivial difference.

**Normality assessment** is required before applying parametric tests. Methods: (1) Visual inspection — histogram showing symmetric bell shape; Q-Q plot (points on the diagonal line); (2) Formal tests — Shapiro-Wilk test (preferred for n<50), Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (larger samples); (3) Descriptive statistics — compare mean and median (similar = symmetric; mean >> median = right skew). Critically: formal normality tests have low power in small samples (type II error — fail to detect non-normality) and excessive power in large samples (reject normality for trivial deviations). For large samples (n>100), visual assessment is more informative than formal tests.

**Multiple comparisons correction** is required when testing more than one hypothesis simultaneously to prevent inflation of the type I error rate. The **Bonferroni correction** divides the significance level α by the number of comparisons (α* = 0.05/k) — this is the most conservative approach and appropriate for a small number of truly independent tests. The **Benjamini-Hochberg (false discovery rate) procedure** is less conservative and more appropriate for exploratory analyses with many comparisons (e.g., genome-wide association studies, proteomic profiling). For pre-specified secondary endpoints in RCTs, a hierarchical testing procedure (gate-keeping) controls error without requiring Bonferroni correction.

Survival analysis handles time-to-event data where not all patients experience the outcome during follow-up (censoring). The **Kaplan-Meier estimator** generates a step-function survival curve by computing the probability of surviving each successive event time. Patients who are lost to follow-up, withdraw, or reach study end without the event are **censored** at their last known alive time — they contribute information up to that point. The **log-rank test** compares survival curves between groups, testing whether the curves are statistically different across the entire follow-up period. The **Cox proportional hazards model** estimates the hazard ratio (HR) — the ratio of the instantaneous risk of the event in one group vs another — after adjusting for covariates. The proportional hazards assumption (constant HR over time) must be verified (Schoenfeld residuals test).

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Interpreting the Hazard Ratio
blocks.content: The hazard ratio is not the same as a relative risk or odds ratio. A HR of 0.65 (95% CI 0.50–0.85) for laparoscopic vs open surgery means that at any given point in time, the rate of the event is 35% lower in the laparoscopic group. The HR is valid only if the proportional hazards assumption holds — if the survival curves cross, the HR is misleading. When survival curves cross, consider restricted mean survival time (RMST) as an alternative summary measure.

**Competing risks** are a critical consideration in surgical oncology survival analysis. A competing risk is any event that prevents the event of interest from occurring or changes the probability of its occurrence. Example: in analysis of disease-specific survival after colorectal cancer resection, death from cardiovascular disease is a competing event — it prevents the patient from dying from colorectal cancer. Standard Kaplan-Meier analysis treating competing events as censored overestimates the cumulative incidence of the event of interest. The **Fine-Gray competing risks model** estimates the subdistribution hazard — the appropriate model when competing events are present — and should be used instead of Kaplan-Meier + Cox when deaths from other causes are common.

**Propensity score analysis** in observational surgical survival studies uses the propensity score — the probability of receiving the treatment given observed covariates — to balance comparison groups. The PS is estimated from logistic regression of treatment on all measured baseline covariates. Balance after matching or weighting is assessed by standardised mean differences (SMD < 0.1 for all covariates indicates adequate balance). Critically: PS methods can only control for **measured confounders** — unmeasured confounders (surgeon skill, performance status not recorded in database) remain. Sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounding using E-values (VanderWeele & Ding, 2017) quantifies how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to explain the observed association.

sections.title: Diagnostic Test Accuracy and Sample Size

blocks.title: Diagnostic Test Accuracy Measures
rows: Ability to detect disease when present
High sensitivity: good for ruling OUT disease (SnNout)
Ability to exclude disease when absent
High specificity: good for ruling IN disease (SpPin)
Probability disease present given positive test
Depends on prevalence — higher in high-prevalence populations
Probability disease absent given negative test
Depends on prevalence — higher in low-prevalence populations
How much more likely positive test in disease
How much less likely negative test in disease

blocks.title: Board Pearl — Sample Size: Type I and Type II Errors
blocks.content: Sample size calculation is determined by four inputs: (1) **α** (Type I error rate = probability of falsely rejecting H₀) — conventionally 0.05; (2) **Power** (1 − β, where β = Type II error rate = probability of missing a true effect) — conventionally 0.80 or 0.90; (3) **Effect size** (the minimum clinically important difference); (4) **Variance** (expected spread of the outcome). Increasing power or reducing α requires a larger sample. An underpowered study cannot reliably detect a true difference — a non-significant result from an underpowered study does not prove equivalence.

sections.title: Multivariate Analysis — Logistic and Linear Regression

blocks.content: **Logistic regression** is used when the outcome is binary (e.g., complication yes/no, 30-day mortality, anastomotic leak). The model estimates the **log-odds** of the outcome as a linear function of predictor variables, producing an **odds ratio (OR)** for each predictor after adjustment for all other variables in the model. The OR represents the multiplicative change in the odds of the outcome per unit increase in the predictor variable (for continuous predictors) or compared to the reference category (for categorical predictors), holding all other predictors constant.

Interpretation of logistic regression results requires attention to: (1) **OR vs RR**: OR is not the same as relative risk (RR). When the outcome is common (>10%), OR substantially overestimates RR and should not be interpreted as if it were a risk ratio. Use RR or risk difference when the outcome is common. (2) **Adjusted vs unadjusted**: the OR from multivariate logistic regression differs from the unadjusted (bivariable) OR — both should be reported, and substantial changes in OR after adjustment indicate confounding. (3) **Model performance**: report Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test and C-statistic (equivalent to AUC) to assess model calibration and discrimination. (4) **Overfitting**: a model with too many predictors relative to events (rule of thumb: 10 events per variable minimum) will overfit — apparent performance is better than true performance in new patients.

blocks.title: Logistic Regression — Common Reporting Elements
rows: Determines maximum model complexity (10 EPV rule)
Primary measure of association after confounding adjustment
OR 2.4 (95% CI 1.3–4.5) for diabetes → 2.4× odds of leak in diabetics
Calibration — does model fit the data well?
Discrimination — does model distinguish cases from non-cases?
Multiple imputation of missing BMI in 12% of patients
Avoid stepwise selection — use clinical a priori or LASSO
Pre-specified clinically based variable selection, not data-driven

blocks.content: **Linear regression** is used when the outcome is a continuous variable (e.g., operative time in minutes, length of stay, haemoglobin change). The model estimates the **mean difference** in outcome per unit change in predictor, producing a **regression coefficient (β)** for each predictor. Assumptions: linearity (association between predictor and outcome is linear); independence of observations; homoscedasticity (constant variance of residuals across fitted values); normality of residuals (not of the outcome). Violations are assessed by residual plots. When the outcome is skewed (e.g., length of stay), log-transformation of the outcome and exponentiation of coefficients produces a geometric mean ratio — more interpretable than the mean difference on a log scale.

blocks.title: Stepwise Variable Selection — A Common Methodological Error
blocks.content: Stepwise variable selection (forward, backward, or stepwise regression) — in which variables are added or removed based on p-values in the model — produces models that are overfit to the sample, have inflated OR estimates, and perform poorly in independent validation. Penalised regression methods (LASSO, ridge, elastic net) are methodologically superior for exploratory prediction modelling. For confirmatory analyses, all variables should be pre-specified based on clinical knowledge and prior evidence — not selected from the data. Reporting 'variables were selected by backward stepwise regression with p<0.05' is a methodological red flag.

Mixed effects models (also called multilevel models, hierarchical models, or random effects models) are used when data have a hierarchical structure — patients nested within hospitals, repeated measurements nested within patients, or assessments nested within cluster-randomised groups. The model contains both **fixed effects** (the average treatment or predictor effect across all groups) and **random effects** (cluster-specific or subject-specific deviations from the overall average). Random effects capture the correlation between observations from the same cluster or subject, producing valid standard errors and inference despite the non-independence.

**Repeated measures designs** collect multiple observations from the same patient over time (e.g., pain scores at day 1, 3, 7, and 30 post-operatively). Standard repeated measures ANOVA assumes sphericity (equal covariance between all time-point pairs) — this assumption is frequently violated in surgical outcome data. **Linear mixed effects models** (LMM) accommodate: (1) unbalanced data (patients with missing time-points are included under missing-at-random assumptions); (2) flexible correlation structures (different correlation patterns between time-points); (3) continuous time measurement (not restricted to pre-specified intervals); (4) time-varying covariates. LMM produces valid inference for outcomes measured longitudinally in surgical trials.

blocks.title: Application — ERAS Protocol Quality of Life Measurement
blocks.content: An RCT of ERAS vs standard care collects quality of life (QoL) at baseline, 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, and 6 months. Mixed effects models allow: (1) including patients with missing time-point measurements (e.g., those who died before 6 months); (2) estimating the trajectory of QoL recovery over time and whether ERAS vs standard care groups differ in trajectory (time-by-group interaction); (3) adjusting for baseline QoL and other covariates. The ANCOVA approach (single time-point analysis adjusting for baseline) is an alternative for the primary endpoint — both approaches have advantages and should be pre-specified.

blocks.title: Mixed Models — Key Applications in Surgical Research
rows: Annual volume, patient risk, procedure year
OR, RR, HR depending on design

blocks.content: Bayesian statistics provides an alternative framework to classical (frequentist) hypothesis testing. Instead of asking 'What is the probability of observing this data if H₀ is true?' (p-value), Bayesian analysis asks 'Given this data, what is the probability that H₁ is true?' — producing a **posterior probability** that the treatment is beneficial. The Bayesian framework formally incorporates **prior beliefs** (previous evidence, expert knowledge) through a **prior distribution**, updates this with the observed data (likelihood), and produces a **posterior distribution** that quantifies uncertainty about the treatment effect.

Key Bayesian outputs in surgical research: (1) **Posterior probability of benefit**: 'There is an 87% probability that laparoscopic resection reduces 30-day complications by more than 5%.' This is directly actionable and clinically intuitive — more interpretable than a p-value. (2) **Credible interval (CrI)**: the Bayesian equivalent of the confidence interval — 'There is a 95% probability that the true treatment effect lies between -2.1% and -8.4% reduction in leak rate.' Unlike frequentist CIs, CrIs have a direct probability interpretation. (3) **Bayes factor (BF)**: the ratio of the marginal likelihood of the data under H₁ versus H₀ — quantifying the strength of evidence for or against H₁. BF > 10 indicates strong evidence for H₁; BF < 0.1 indicates strong evidence for H₀; BF between 0.1 and 10 is inconclusive.

**Prior distribution selection** is the most contentious aspect of Bayesian analysis. Choices: (1) **Informative prior**: based on prior meta-analytic evidence or mechanistic knowledge — appropriate when substantial relevant prior evidence exists; (2) **Weakly informative prior**: centred on no effect with broad dispersion, allowing the data to dominate — appropriate when prior evidence is weak or there is concern about prior influence on conclusions; (3) **Non-informative (flat) prior**: equal probability across all possible values — produces results similar to frequentist analysis; (4) **Sceptical prior**: centred slightly against the intervention being beneficial — appropriate in a confirmatory analysis to guard against over-interpretation. Prior sensitivity analysis should always be reported: if conclusions change substantially with different reasonable priors, the evidence is not robust.

blocks.title: MCMC — Markov Chain Monte Carlo Sampling
blocks.content: For complex Bayesian models, the posterior distribution cannot be computed analytically — it is approximated by **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling**. MCMC iteratively generates random samples from the posterior distribution; once the chain converges, these samples represent draws from the posterior and can be summarised (mean, credible interval). MCMC convergence must be verified by: trace plots (should show stationary behaviour, not trending); R-hat statistic (<1.1 for all parameters indicates convergence); effective sample size (>1000 for reliable posterior estimates). Common MCMC software: Stan (via RStan or CmdStan), BUGS/JAGS, PyMC. Bayesian adaptive trial designs use MCMC to update posterior probabilities at each interim analysis.

sections.title: Diagnostic Test Accuracy — ROC Curves and AUC

blocks.content: The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** plots sensitivity (true positive rate) on the y-axis against 1−specificity (false positive rate) on the x-axis across all possible decision thresholds for a continuous diagnostic test or prediction model. Each point on the curve represents a specific threshold — moving along the curve corresponds to shifting the decision threshold from stringent (high specificity, low sensitivity — lower right of curve) to permissive (high sensitivity, low specificity — upper left of curve). A perfect test occupies the top-left corner (sensitivity=1.0, 1−specificity=0); a useless test lies along the diagonal (AUC=0.5).

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)**, also termed the C-statistic in logistic regression, is the single summary measure of a diagnostic test's discriminative ability. AUC = 0.5 corresponds to no discriminative ability (equivalent to random assignment). AUC interpretation for surgical risk models: 0.5–0.6 poor; 0.6–0.7 fair; 0.7–0.8 acceptable; 0.8–0.9 excellent; 0.9–1.0 outstanding. The clinical utility of a model with a given AUC depends on the decision context — an AUC of 0.75 for perioperative mortality prediction may be clinically useful despite moderate discrimination. AUC does not reflect calibration (how close predicted probabilities are to observed probabilities) — always report both AUC and calibration assessment.

**Optimal threshold selection** from a ROC curve should be guided by clinical context, not purely by statistical criteria. Common approaches: (1) **Youden's J statistic** (maximises sensitivity + specificity − 1) — minimises total misclassification; (2) **Minimum distance to top-left corner** — finds the threshold closest to the ideal point; (3) **Clinical utility analysis** — weights false negatives and false positives differentially based on clinical consequences. Example: in pre-operative risk stratification for major surgery, the consequence of a false negative (failing to identify a high-risk patient) is worse than a false positive (unnecessarily optimising a low-risk patient) — the threshold should be set with higher sensitivity at the cost of specificity.

blocks.title: ROC Analysis — Key Metrics for Surgical Prediction Models
rows: Agreement between predicted and observed rates
POSSUM overpredicts mortality in low-risk patients
Improvement in classification with new model vs old
Adding serum albumin improves POSSUM NRI
Adding frailty score to ASA grade improves IDI
Net benefit at each threshold probability
Superior net benefit across clinical range
Optimal threshold for ERAS candidacy decision

blocks.title: Internal vs External Validation
blocks.content: A prediction model's AUC computed in the same dataset used for development (apparent AUC) is always optimistic — it reflects overfitting, not true performance. Internal validation methods (bootstrap, cross-validation) correct for optimism and produce a shrinkage factor to adjust coefficients. However, internal validation does not address generalisability. **External validation** in an independent cohort from a different setting and time period is the gold standard for demonstrating clinical utility. The TRIPOD statement (Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis Or Diagnosis) mandates reporting of both development and validation performance metrics.

sections.title: Missing Data — MCAR, MAR, MNAR, and Imputation

blocks.content: Missing data are ubiquitous in surgical research — up to 30–40% of patients in database studies may have missing values for one or more variables. The approach to missing data determines whether analysis results are valid or biased. The mechanism of missingness — why data are missing — is critical. Three mechanisms are defined: (1) **Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)**: missingness is unrelated to any observed or unobserved variable — essentially random; (2) **Missing At Random (MAR)**: missingness depends on observed variables but not on the unobserved value of the missing variable itself — missingness can be explained by other data in the dataset; (3) **Missing Not At Random (MNAR)**: missingness depends on the missing value itself — e.g., patients with high BMI do not have BMI recorded because it is not measured in morbidly obese patients. MNAR is the most problematic mechanism and cannot be resolved by standard imputation.

**Multiple imputation** (MI) is the recommended approach for handling missing data under MCAR and MAR mechanisms. MI: (1) creates multiple (typically m=20) completed datasets by sampling from the predictive distribution of missing values given observed data; (2) analyses each completed dataset using the planned analysis; (3) combines results using Rubin's rules (pooled estimates and standard errors that account for imputation uncertainty). MI is implemented in all major statistical software (R: mice package; Stata: mi impute; SAS: PROC MI). The imputation model should include all variables in the analysis model plus any auxiliary variables correlated with missingness — omitting a variable from the imputation model that is included in the analysis model biases results.

blocks.title: Missing Data Mechanisms — Summary and Approach
rows: BMI missing because laboratory tube was lost
Complete case analysis valid; MI increases efficiency
Missingness depends on observed data, not on missing value
BMI missing more in elderly patients (but BMI itself doesn't predict missingness)
Multiple imputation with appropriate imputation model
Missingness depends on the unobserved missing value
Complications not recorded because patient died before assessment
Sensitivity analysis under different MNAR assumptions; pattern mixture models
Patient stops responding to QoL questionnaires after complications
Mixed models (MAR assumption) + MNAR sensitivity analysis

blocks.title: Complete Case Analysis — When It Is Valid
blocks.content: Complete case analysis (CCA) — analysing only patients with complete data — is valid only under the MCAR mechanism. Under MAR, CCA produces biased estimates because missingness is systematically related to covariates. In most surgical database studies, data are missing for systematic reasons (older patients, emergency presentations, early years of registry data) — making CCA biased. The fraction of missing information (not just the percentage of cases with missing data) determines the impact on standard errors. Multiple imputation should be the default approach for any dataset with >5% missing data in key variables, reported alongside CCA as a sensitivity analysis.

consolidation.intro: Test your biostatistics knowledge with 20 questions.

questions.stem: A retrospective cohort study compares 30-day morbidity after open versus laparoscopic colectomy. Hospital length of stay (LOS) is the primary outcome. Histogram analysis shows LOS is right-skewed (median 6 days, mean 9 days, range 3–68 days). Which statistical test is most appropriate for comparing LOS between groups?

options.A: Independent samples t-test — comparing means of two independent groups
options.B: Paired t-test — comparing pre- and post-operative measurements
options.C: Mann-Whitney U test — comparing two independent groups with non-normal distribution
options.D: Chi-squared test — comparing categorical outcomes between two groups

explanation.correct: Length of stay is a continuous variable with a right-skewed distribution (mean >> median; long upper tail due to outliers). The t-test assumes normal distribution — applying it to skewed data produces invalid p-values. The Mann-Whitney U test (Wilcoxon rank-sum test) is the non-parametric equivalent, comparing rank distributions without normality assumption. Log-transformation followed by t-test on log-transformed values is a valid alternative.

wrong.A: The independent t-test requires approximately normal distribution. With severe skewness (mean 9, median 6, range 3–68), normality is violated, invalidating the result.
wrong.B: Paired t-test is for matched or repeated-measure data (same patient before and after), not for comparing two separate patient groups.
wrong.D: Chi-squared test is for categorical outcomes. LOS is continuous — applying chi-squared requires arbitrary categorisation that discards information.

explanation.guideline: STROBE 2007 — Item 12: describe statistical methods; specify whether normality was checked before applying parametric tests
explanation.trial: ERAS programme trials (Kehlet H, Ann Surg 2008): typically use Mann-Whitney U for LOS comparisons due to characteristic right-skewed distribution.
explanation.pearl: Whenever mean >> median in surgical outcome data (LOS, blood loss, operative time), suspect right skewness — use non-parametric tests or log-transformation rather than t-test.

questions.stem: In a study of 500 patients, BMI data are missing in 80 patients (16%) because older patients in the early years of the registry were not routinely weighed. BMI is strongly associated with the primary outcome (anastomotic leak). Which missing data mechanism BEST describes this situation?

options.A: Missing Completely At Random (MCAR) — the missing data are unrelated to any measured variable
options.B: Missing At Random (MAR) — missingness depends on patient age (an observed variable) but not on BMI value itself
options.C: Missing Not At Random (MNAR) — patients with higher BMI are specifically less likely to have BMI recorded
options.D: Informative censoring — patients withdrew from the study before BMI measurement

explanation.correct: The missingness of BMI depends on patient age and registration year — both observed variables in the dataset. This is the definition of Missing At Random (MAR): missingness depends on observed variables but not on the unobserved value of the missing variable itself. Under MAR, multiple imputation using age, year, and other observed covariates as auxiliary variables will produce valid estimates. Complete case analysis would be biased here because older patients (with missing BMI) systematically differ from younger patients.

wrong.A: MCAR requires that missingness is completely unrelated to any data in the dataset — the relationship between missingness and patient age/year rules this out.
wrong.C: MNAR would require that specifically obese patients (high BMI) are more likely to have BMI missing — this is not what the question describes. The missingness depends on era and age, not on the BMI value.
wrong.D: Informative censoring is a survival analysis concept — it describes differential dropout related to prognosis in time-to-event analyses, not a mechanism for cross-sectional missing covariate data.

explanation.guideline: Sterne JAC et al. 'Multiple imputation for missing data in epidemiological and clinical research.' BMJ 2009 — practical guide to MAR/MCAR/MNAR and imputation
explanation.trial: National Bowel Cancer Audit (NBOCA): Missing BMI is a recognised problem in early registry years — MAR assumption supported by systematic analysis of predictors of missingness.
explanation.pearl: To determine the missing data mechanism: model missingness as an outcome using logistic regression — if observed variables (age, year, centre) predict missingness, MAR is plausible and multiple imputation is appropriate.

questions.stem: A surgical prediction model for postoperative pneumonia after oesophagectomy achieves an AUC of 0.83 in the development cohort and 0.74 in an independent validation cohort. What is the most appropriate interpretation?

options.A: The model performs identically in both cohorts — AUC of 0.83 and 0.74 are statistically equivalent
options.B: The model shows acceptable discrimination in development (0.83 = excellent) with some optimism; the externally validated AUC of 0.74 (acceptable discrimination) is the appropriate estimate of real-world performance
options.C: The model is clinically invalid because external validation AUC is lower than development AUC
options.D: The model should be re-developed in the validation cohort to achieve AUC ≥0.80

explanation.correct: A decrease in AUC from development to external validation (shrinkage) is expected and not a model failure — it reflects correction for overfitting. The development AUC is always optimistic because the model was fitted to the same data it is evaluated on. External validation in an independent cohort provides the unbiased estimate of real-world performance. An externally validated AUC of 0.74 is clinically acceptable for a perioperative risk prediction model and is the figure that should be reported as the model's performance for clinical use.

wrong.A: A difference of 0.09 in AUC between development and validation is clinically and statistically meaningful — the two values are not equivalent.
wrong.C: Optimism (development > validation AUC) is universal and expected. It does not invalidate the model. The threshold for clinical utility is the validation AUC, not whether shrinkage occurred.
wrong.D: Refitting the model in the validation cohort conflates development and validation, destroying the independent assessment. If the validation AUC is unacceptably low, the model requires re-development in a new larger dataset and re-validation in yet another independent cohort.

explanation.guideline: TRIPOD 2015 — Items 10b, 14b: report and compare development and validation model performance; internal and external validation required for model endorsement
explanation.trial: Protopapa KL et al. P-POSSUM (Br J Surg 2014): external validation of P-POSSUM across UK hospitals showed AUC 0.81 — appropriate external validation methodology.
explanation.pearl: The externally validated AUC, not the development AUC, is what goes on your model's performance certificate. Report both, but emphasise the external validation result as the clinically relevant figure.

questions.stem: In a multivariate logistic regression model predicting anastomotic leak after low anterior resection, diabetes is associated with OR 2.4 (95% CI 1.3–4.5). The baseline (non-diabetic) anastomotic leak rate is 8%. What does this OR most accurately represent in clinical terms?

options.A: Diabetic patients have a 2.4 times higher probability of anastomotic leak than non-diabetics
options.B: The odds of anastomotic leak are 2.4 times higher in diabetic patients than non-diabetic patients, after adjusting for all other variables in the model
options.C: Diabetic patients have a 140% absolute increase in anastomotic leak rate compared to non-diabetics
options.D: For every diabetic patient, 2.4 anastomotic leaks occur per non-diabetic patient's leak rate

explanation.correct: The adjusted OR from logistic regression represents the ratio of the odds of the outcome in diabetic versus non-diabetic patients, after adjustment for all other variables in the model. Odds (not probability) is the ratio of events to non-events: at 8% baseline leak rate, odds = 0.08/0.92 = 0.087. Odds in diabetics = 0.087 × 2.4 = 0.208, corresponding to a risk of 0.208/1.208 = 17.2%. Note: because the outcome (8% leak rate) is moderately common, the OR overestimates the relative risk (RR = 17.2%/8% = 2.15, not 2.4). The adjusted qualification is critical — it distinguishes multivariable results from crude associations.

wrong.A: Odds ratio is not the same as probability ratio (relative risk). OR overestimates RR when the outcome is common. 2.4 times higher probability would be a relative risk, which differs from the OR.
wrong.C: A 140% absolute increase would mean leak rate = 8% + 140% = >100%, which is impossible. The absolute risk increase calculated from OR 2.4 at 8% baseline is approximately 9.2 percentage points (not 140% absolute).
wrong.D: The OR is a ratio of odds, not a rate per individual. This interpretation is numerically and conceptually incorrect.

explanation.guideline: STROBE 2007 Item 16a — report adjusted and unadjusted estimates; note that OR overestimates RR for common outcomes
explanation.trial: Frasson M et al. (Br J Surg 2015): multivariate analysis of anastomotic leak after left colectomy — OR for diabetes 2.1 (95% CI 1.1–4.0) after adjustment for bowel preparation, ASA grade, and stapler type.
explanation.pearl: When the outcome proportion in the reference group exceeds 10%, OR substantially overestimates RR. Report risk differences and use methods that estimate RR directly (log-binomial or Poisson regression) for common binary outcomes.

questions.stem: A Cox proportional hazards model for overall survival after pancreatic resection has a Schoenfeld residuals test result of p=0.002 for the predictor 'node-positive disease.' What does this finding indicate?

options.A: Node-positive disease is strongly associated with poor survival — the low p-value confirms significance
options.B: The proportional hazards assumption is violated for node-positive disease — the hazard ratio changes over time, making the single HR an unreliable summary of the survival difference
options.C: The model has insufficient sample size for reliable HR estimation
options.D: Schoenfeld residuals are used to test model calibration, not proportional hazards

explanation.correct: The Schoenfeld residuals test evaluates the proportional hazards (PH) assumption — that the HR between groups is constant over time. A p-value of 0.002 indicates significant violation of the PH assumption for node-positive disease: the hazard ratio of node-positive vs node-negative disease changes over the follow-up period (e.g., survival curves converge or cross over time). Reporting a single HR when the PH assumption is violated is misleading. Solutions: (1) report HR separately for different time periods; (2) use restricted mean survival time (RMST) as an alternative summary measure; (3) add a time-varying covariate for node status × time interaction in the Cox model.

wrong.A: The p-value from the Schoenfeld test is for the proportional hazards assumption, not for the significance of the association between node status and survival. These are entirely different p-values.
wrong.C: Schoenfeld residuals test is unrelated to sample size adequacy — it tests the PH assumption only.
wrong.D: Schoenfeld residuals specifically test the proportional hazards assumption in Cox regression. Calibration of survival models is tested using different methods (e.g., calibration plots, Gronnesby-Borgan test).

explanation.guideline: Schoenfeld D. 'Partial residuals for the proportional hazards regression model.' Biometrika 1982 — original paper; STROBE Item 12b requires documentation of proportional hazards assumption testing
explanation.trial: ESPAC-4 (gemcitabine + capecitabine vs gemcitabine for pancreatic cancer): careful Cox model assumption testing identified early non-proportional hazards — RMST analysis reported alongside HR.
explanation.pearl: Always test the proportional hazards assumption (Schoenfeld residuals) before reporting Cox model results. If violated, plot the log-log survival curves and report time-period specific HRs or RMST.

questions.stem: A surgeon reports that a new intraoperative fluorescence imaging technique for bile duct identification achieves sensitivity 0.94 and specificity 0.71 in a validation study. Applying this to a population where 5% of laparoscopic cholecystectomies involve an aberrant biliary anatomy requiring identification, what is the positive predictive value (PPV)?

explanation.correct: PPV = TP / (TP + FP). Using Bayes' theorem: PPV = (Sensitivity × Prevalence) / [(Sensitivity × Prevalence) + (1−Specificity) × (1−Prevalence)]. With Sensitivity = 0.94, Specificity = 0.71, Prevalence = 0.05: PPV = (0.94 × 0.05) / [(0.94 × 0.05) + (0.29 × 0.95)] = 0.047 / (0.047 + 0.2755) = 0.047 / 0.3225 = 0.146 ≈ 0.149. Despite excellent sensitivity, the low prevalence of aberrant anatomy (5%) means that most positive tests are false positives — the technique identifies the truly aberrant anatomy in only ~15% of positive alerts. This illustrates the prevalence-dependence of PPV.

wrong.A: 0.94 is the sensitivity, not the PPV. Sensitivity measures performance in patients who truly have the condition; PPV applies to patients who test positive.
wrong.B: 0.71 is the specificity. Specificity measures performance in patients without the condition.
wrong.D: 0.997 would be close to the NPV — in this low-prevalence setting, a negative test is very reassuring (high NPV). NPV = (Specificity × (1−Prevalence)) / [(Specificity × (1−Prevalence)) + (1−Sensitivity) × Prevalence] ≈ 0.996.

explanation.guideline: STARD 2015 — Standards for Reporting Diagnostic accuracy Studies; Item 20: report sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV with CIs; note prevalence-dependence of PPV/NPV
explanation.trial: FALCON trial (fluorescence cholangiography in cholecystectomy): demonstrated high sensitivity (0.91) but PPV was prevalence-dependent — highlighted importance of reporting performance at population prevalence, not just in enriched validation cohorts.
explanation.pearl: PPV and NPV are highly dependent on prevalence. A test with excellent sensitivity and specificity may have poor PPV in low-prevalence populations (and excellent NPV). Likelihood ratios (LR+ and LR−) are prevalence-independent measures of diagnostic performance — prefer them for cross-population comparison.

questions.stem: A researcher conducts a Bayesian re-analysis of a negative surgical RCT (p=0.12 favouring the intervention) using a weakly informative prior centred on no effect. The posterior probability that the treatment benefit exceeds the minimally important clinical difference is 72%. What is the most appropriate interpretation?

options.A: The trial was statistically significant — a 72% posterior probability confirms superiority
options.B: The trial shows moderate evidence favouring the intervention, but the posterior probability of benefit (72%) does not reach the threshold typically considered convincing (≥95%) — further evidence is needed
options.C: The Bayesian re-analysis contradicts the frequentist result — the frequentist p=0.12 should be disregarded
options.D: A 72% posterior probability of benefit proves the null hypothesis is false

explanation.correct: Bayesian posterior probability interpretation: 72% probability of benefit means there is a 28% probability that the treatment does NOT provide clinically meaningful benefit. This is not conclusive evidence of efficacy. While this is more informative than a p=0.12 (which only says 'not quite significant under strict frequentist rules'), a 72% posterior probability is typically insufficient to change clinical practice — most clinicians and guideline bodies require ≥90–95% posterior probability before recommending a new surgical intervention, particularly given the costs and risks involved. The Bayesian re-analysis and the frequentist result are actually concordant — both suggest a plausible benefit that is not definitively proven.

wrong.A: 72% posterior probability is not the equivalent of statistical significance. Most Bayesian frameworks require ≥90–95% posterior probability for actionable evidence of benefit.
wrong.C: Bayesian and frequentist analyses answer slightly different questions but are not contradictory — a 72% posterior probability is compatible with a frequentist p=0.12 (both indicate insufficient evidence for definitive conclusions).
wrong.D: 72% posterior probability means 28% probability of no clinically meaningful benefit — the null hypothesis is certainly not proven false with this level of evidence.

explanation.guideline: FDA guidance on Bayesian statistical methods for medical devices (2010); Bland JM & Altman DG. 'Bayesians and frequentists.' BMJ 1998
explanation.trial: REMAP-CAP trial (corticosteroids in COVID-19 critical illness): reported posterior probability of superiority >99% at early adaptive stopping — an example of compelling Bayesian evidence in a platform trial.
explanation.pearl: The key advantage of Bayesian posterior probability is direct interpretability: 'there is a 72% chance this treatment is beneficial' is more clinically actionable than 'p=0.12 does not reach significance.' But the threshold for action must still be pre-specified — 72% is rarely sufficient.

questions.stem: A multicentre cohort study of laparoscopic vs open gastrectomy uses mixed effects logistic regression with hospital as a random effect. What does the random effect for hospital capture in this analysis?

options.A: The average treatment effect of laparoscopic vs open surgery across all hospitals
options.B: Between-hospital variability in baseline complication rates, independent of the treatment effect and patient covariates
options.C: The number of patients per hospital
options.D: The quality score of each participating hospital

explanation.correct: A random effect for hospital in a mixed effects model captures the residual between-hospital variability in baseline outcome rates (complication rates) that remains after accounting for the treatment effect and patient-level covariates. Each hospital has an estimated random intercept — a hospital-specific deviation from the overall mean log-odds of the outcome. Including this random effect: (1) accounts for the correlation between patients within the same hospital (within-cluster correlation); (2) adjusts the treatment effect estimate for between-hospital confounding; (3) produces valid standard errors that reflect the hierarchical data structure. The fixed effect for treatment (laparoscopic vs open) gives the average treatment effect across all hospitals.

wrong.A: The average treatment effect is captured by the fixed effect coefficient for the treatment variable, not the random effect.
wrong.C: Hospital sample size (n per hospital) is a model weight consideration, not what the random effect parameter represents.
wrong.D: Hospital quality is not directly captured by a random intercept — it is an unmeasured hospital-level factor that contributes to the between-hospital variance captured by the random effect.

explanation.guideline: Bates D et al. 'Fitting linear mixed-effects models using lme4.' J Stat Softw 2015; CONSORT cluster RCT extension — random effects for cluster accounting required
explanation.trial: Tekkis PP et al. (Ann Surg 2003): random effects model for hospital volume-outcomes in colorectal surgery — hospital random effects captured substantial between-hospital variability in mortality independent of case mix.
explanation.pearl: The intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) from a mixed effects model — calculated as the variance of the random effect divided by total variance — quantifies how much of the outcome variance is attributable to between-cluster differences. ICC > 0.05 indicates clinically meaningful clustering that must be accounted for in sample size calculations.

questions.stem: A competing risks analysis is preferred over standard Kaplan-Meier analysis for cumulative incidence estimation when:

options.A: The sample size is large (>1000 patients)
options.B: Events of interest are rare (<5% cumulative incidence)
options.C: Patients can die from causes other than the outcome of interest, and these deaths preclude the event of interest

explanation.correct: Competing risks analysis is required when deaths from causes other than the outcome of interest compete with occurrence of that outcome. Standard Kaplan-Meier treats competing events (deaths from other causes) as censored, implicitly assuming they have no effect on the probability of the outcome of interest — this leads to overestimation of cumulative incidence, particularly in elderly surgical populations with high competing mortality. The Fine-Gray subdistribution hazard model or the cause-specific hazard model correctly handles competing events. Example: cumulative incidence of local recurrence after colon cancer resection — deaths from cardiovascular disease prevent patients from experiencing local recurrence; ignoring these competing deaths overestimates the true recurrence risk.

wrong.A: Competing risks analysis is not determined by sample size — it is determined by the data structure (presence of events that prevent the outcome of interest).
wrong.B: Rare outcomes require appropriate statistical methods for precision (exact methods, penalised regression), but competing risks analysis is not specifically indicated by outcome rarity.
wrong.D: Follow-up duration alone does not indicate the need for competing risks analysis — the presence of competing events does.

explanation.guideline: Dignam JJ et al. 'Use of competing risks analysis in cancer research.' J Clin Oncol 2012; Fine JP & Gray RJ. 'A proportional hazards model for the subdistribution of a competing risk.' JASA 1999
explanation.trial: MOSAIC trial (FOLFOX vs LV5FU2 for colon cancer): competing risks analysis for disease-free survival used to account for cardiovascular deaths in an elderly population — standard KM overestimated recurrence risk.
explanation.pearl: In any oncology survival analysis of elderly patients with comorbidities, check: what fraction of 'censored' patients died from non-cancer causes? If substantial (>10%), competing risks analysis is required and KM-based cumulative incidence estimates should not be used.

questions.stem: A study reports that laparoscopic versus open colectomy is associated with significantly lower 30-day complication rate (18% vs 26%, p=0.001). The 95% CI for the difference is −12.0% to −3.5%. Which statement BEST describes the relationship between statistical and clinical significance?

options.A: Statistical significance (p=0.001) confirms clinical significance — the result is definitely clinically meaningful
options.B: The result is statistically significant; the 95% CI suggests the absolute reduction in complications ranges from 3.5% to 12.0% — clinical significance depends on whether this magnitude is important to patients and surgeons
options.C: A p-value of 0.001 is too small — this indicates data fabrication
options.D: Clinical significance is determined by whether the p-value is less than 0.001

explanation.correct: Statistical significance (p<0.05) and clinical significance are distinct concepts. Statistical significance indicates that the observed difference is unlikely to be due to chance — it does not indicate the magnitude or importance of the difference. The 95% CI (−12% to −3.5%) shows the range of plausible absolute treatment effects — at the lower bound, a 3.5 percentage point reduction in complications may be clinically marginal (especially if laparoscopic surgery has higher costs or longer operative times); at the upper bound, 12 percentage points would be very clinically meaningful. Whether 3.5–12% reduction in complications justifies the change requires consideration of patient preferences, costs, learning curve, and institutional capability.

wrong.A: Statistical significance is a statement about chance variability, not about the clinical importance of the finding. Large samples can produce statistically significant but trivially small differences.
wrong.C: p=0.001 is a legitimately achievable p-value in large, well-designed studies — it does not indicate fabrication.
wrong.D: Clinical significance is a clinical judgement about the importance of the effect size, not a statistical threshold. Some fields use p<0.001 as a more stringent criterion for strong evidence, but this is still a statistical, not clinical, threshold.

explanation.guideline: CONSORT 2010 Item 17: report effect size with CIs; encourage clinical interpretation beyond p-value
explanation.trial: LANDMARK trial (laparoscopic vs open gastrectomy): p=0.04 for overall survival — absolute survival difference was 2.4%, considered minimally clinically significant by many commentators despite statistical significance.
explanation.pearl: Never interpret a p-value without the effect size and confidence interval. A p=0.001 with CI −0.5% to −3% is clinically trivial; a p=0.04 with CI −8% to −0.2% may be clinically important. The clinical question is always: 'Is this magnitude of difference important enough to change practice?'

questions.stem: A researcher wants to assess the assumption of normality for a continuous outcome variable (wound complication score) before applying a parametric test. The sample size is n=35. Which method is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (large sample version) with p<0.05 indicating normal distribution
options.B: Shapiro-Wilk test combined with visual assessment of the histogram and Q-Q plot
options.C: If n>30, normality can be assumed based on the central limit theorem without formal testing
options.D: Formal normality testing is unnecessary — always use parametric tests regardless of distribution

explanation.correct: The Shapiro-Wilk test has the highest power among formal normality tests for small samples (n<50) and is the preferred formal test. However, formal tests should always be combined with visual assessment: a histogram showing the distribution shape and a Q-Q plot (quantile-quantile plot where points on the diagonal indicate normality). For n=35, Shapiro-Wilk has reasonable power but may miss moderate non-normality — visual assessment adds important information about the nature of any deviation (skewness, heavy tails, bimodality). A non-significant Shapiro-Wilk (p>0.05) combined with a visually approximately normal histogram supports parametric analysis.

wrong.A: Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test is less powerful than Shapiro-Wilk for small samples (n<50) and is more appropriate for larger samples. Critically, a non-significant K-S test does not confirm normality — it only fails to reject it. The interpretation should be that non-normality was not detected, not that normality is confirmed.
wrong.C: The central limit theorem applies to the sampling distribution of the mean — ensuring that the mean follows approximately normal distribution for large samples. However, for small samples (n=35), the CLT approximation may not be adequate, particularly for heavily skewed outcomes. Normality of the data (not just the mean) is the assumption of the t-test.
wrong.D: Blindly applying parametric tests without normality assessment is methodologically incorrect and can produce invalid inference for skewed surgical outcomes.

explanation.guideline: Altman DG (1991) Practical Statistics for Medical Research — Chapter 4: normality testing and transformation
explanation.trial: ERAS meta-analyses consistently show right-skewed LOS data — illustrating that formal normality assessment is essential before test selection in surgical research.
explanation.pearl: For n>100, formal normality tests (Shapiro-Wilk, K-S) are often significant for trivial deviations from normality due to high power. For large samples, visual assessment (histogram, Q-Q plot) is more informative for deciding whether the deviation is large enough to warrant non-parametric methods.

questions.stem: A RCT of enhanced pain management after major colorectal surgery measures pain scores at 4 time points (day 1, 3, 7, and 30 post-operatively). Some patients have missing assessments at intermediate time points due to hospital re-admissions. Which statistical approach is MOST appropriate for the primary analysis?

options.A: Repeated measures ANOVA using only complete cases (patients with all 4 time-point measurements)
options.B: Separate t-test at each time point with Bonferroni correction
options.C: Linear mixed effects model including all randomised patients, with time and treatment×time interaction as fixed effects and patient as a random effect
options.D: Last observation carried forward (LOCF) imputation followed by repeated measures ANOVA

explanation.correct: Linear mixed effects models (LMM) handle repeated measures data with several advantages over repeated measures ANOVA: (1) include all randomised patients with any available data, not just complete cases — under the MAR assumption, this produces unbiased estimates; (2) accommodate unequal spacing of time points; (3) allow flexible covariance structures; (4) naturally include the treatment × time interaction to test whether trajectories of pain recovery differ between groups. The primary clinical question — does the intervention alter the trajectory of pain recovery over time? — is directly answered by the treatment × time interaction term.

wrong.A: Complete case ANOVA discards data from patients with any missing time point, potentially biasing results if missingness is related to treatment or outcome (MAR). It also assumes sphericity (equal correlations between time points), which is rarely satisfied.
wrong.B: Separate t-tests at each time point require Bonferroni correction and address independent cross-sectional questions at each time — they do not model the trajectory of recovery or use the within-patient correlation to improve efficiency.
wrong.D: LOCF is a discredited missing data method — it assumes the last observation is unchanged after the measurement point, which is implausible in a recovery trajectory. It can bias results toward or away from the null depending on the direction of the trajectory.

explanation.guideline: ICH E9(R1) — recommends LMM as the estimand-consistent analysis for longitudinal clinical trial data; Fitzmaurice GM et al. 'Applied Longitudinal Analysis' — standard reference
explanation.trial: RECOVERY trial post-ICU (Denehy et al.): mixed effects models with time × treatment interaction used for primary analysis of physical function recovery — included patients with intermediate missing assessments.
explanation.pearl: Pre-specify the covariance structure for the LMM in the statistical analysis plan. The unstructured covariance structure is the most flexible but uses the most parameters — for surgical trials with 3–5 time points, compound symmetry or AR(1) covariance structures are often appropriate and more parsimonious.

questions.stem: In a study examining the relationship between surgical volume (cases per year) and 30-day mortality after oesophagectomy, the researchers apply a Bonferroni correction. The original α is 0.05, and they are testing 10 pre-specified hypotheses. What is the Bonferroni-corrected significance threshold?

explanation.correct: The Bonferroni correction sets the significance threshold for each individual test as α/k, where k is the number of independent comparisons. With α=0.05 and k=10 pre-specified hypotheses: α* = 0.05/10 = 0.005. Each individual test must achieve p<0.005 to be declared significant at the familywise error rate of α=0.05. The Bonferroni correction is the most conservative multiplicity correction — it controls the familywise error rate (probability of any false positive across all tests) at the specified α. It is appropriate when all k hypotheses are independent and of equal importance.

wrong.A: p<0.05 is the uncorrected threshold. Using this for 10 comparisons gives an expected 0.5 false positives per study — not adequate control of type I error.
wrong.C: p<0.001 would be an over-correction (α/50 = 0.001, applied to 50 comparisons, not 10). For 10 comparisons, p<0.005 is the correct Bonferroni threshold.
wrong.D: p<0.10 would be less stringent than the uncorrected threshold — this would increase the type I error rate, not control it.

explanation.guideline: CONSORT 2010 Item 12b — for multiple primary endpoints or subgroup analyses, specify any multiplicity correction used in the statistical analysis plan
explanation.trial: Birkmeyer JD et al. (NEJM 2002): volume-outcomes analysis in oesophagectomy — used appropriate multiple comparison adjustments for simultaneous assessment of multiple volume thresholds.
explanation.pearl: Bonferroni is appropriate for a small number of pre-specified independent hypotheses. For many correlated tests (e.g., multiple secondary endpoints that share the same patients), Bonferroni is overly conservative — consider hierarchical testing or Benjamini-Hochberg FDR instead.

questions.stem: Propensity score matching is used in an observational study of open versus robotic pancreaticoduodenectomy. After matching on 15 covariates, standardised mean differences (SMD) for all covariates are <0.1. What does this indicate?

options.A: The matched groups are statistically significantly different on all covariates
options.B: The propensity score model contains too many covariates for the available sample size
options.C: Covariate balance is adequate — matched groups are comparable on all measured baseline characteristics, reducing confounding by measured variables
options.D: The study is now equivalent to a randomised controlled trial

explanation.correct: Standardised mean difference (SMD) < 0.1 is the conventional threshold for adequate covariate balance after propensity score matching. SMD = (mean_treated − mean_control) / pooled_SD — it is scale-free and can be compared across variables measured on different scales. SMD < 0.1 for all matched covariates indicates that the matched groups are well-balanced on measured characteristics, substantially reducing confounding by those variables. This is assessed graphically with a 'love plot' showing pre- and post-matching SMDs.

wrong.A: SMD < 0.1 indicates adequate balance (small difference), not significant difference. P-values are not recommended for assessing balance in propensity-matched samples — they depend on sample size and can be significant even for trivially small differences in large samples.
wrong.B: 15 covariates in a propensity score model is often appropriate for large datasets. Model complexity is assessed by the c-statistic of the propensity model (it should be neither too high [near 1.0] nor too low [near 0.5]).
wrong.D: Propensity score matching cannot randomise unmeasured confounders. Residual confounding from variables not included in the propensity model always remains. The study is observational, not equivalent to an RCT.

explanation.guideline: Austin PC. 'An introduction to propensity score methods for reducing the effects of confounding in observational studies.' Multivariate Behav Res 2011 — SMD <0.1 benchmark
explanation.trial: Booka E et al. (Ann Surg 2021): propensity-matched comparison of robotic vs open oesophagectomy — SMD <0.1 for all covariates confirmed adequate balance before outcome comparison.
explanation.pearl: Always report SMDs (not p-values) for covariate balance after propensity score matching. A 'love plot' showing pre- and post-matching SMDs is the gold standard for balance reporting. Include it in supplementary materials of any propensity score analysis.

questions.stem: A clinical prediction model for 90-day mortality after major abdominal surgery achieves AUC=0.79. The Hosmer-Lemeshow (HL) goodness-of-fit test gives p=0.003. How should these results be interpreted?

options.A: The model has acceptable discrimination (AUC=0.79) but poor calibration (HL p=0.003 — observed and predicted rates disagree significantly)
options.B: The model has poor discrimination and excellent calibration — the p=0.003 confirms the model fits perfectly
options.C: Both AUC and HL test confirm the model is ready for clinical implementation
options.D: AUC=0.79 is too low for clinical use — the model should be discarded regardless of calibration

explanation.correct: AUC and Hosmer-Lemeshow (HL) test assess different dimensions of model performance. AUC = 0.79 indicates acceptable discrimination — the model ranks patients correctly approximately 79% of the time. HL p=0.003 (significant) indicates poor calibration — there are statistically significant systematic differences between predicted and observed outcome rates in at least some risk deciles. The model may, for example, overpredict mortality in high-risk patients and underpredict in low-risk patients. For clinical use (e.g., risk-adjusted outcome benchmarking), both adequate discrimination AND adequate calibration are required. Poor calibration should prompt recalibration (adjusting intercept and slope) before clinical deployment.

wrong.A: This is the correct answer — discrimination is a separate and complementary measure from calibration.
wrong.B: HL p=0.003 (significant) indicates POOR calibration — a significant HL p-value means the model's predicted probabilities deviate significantly from observed rates. A non-significant HL (p>0.05) would indicate adequate calibration.
wrong.C: Poor calibration (HL p=0.003) indicates the model should not be implemented as-is. Recalibration is needed.
wrong.D: AUC=0.79 is clinically acceptable discrimination for a perioperative risk model — this alone is not a reason to discard the model. The calibration issue is the primary concern.

explanation.guideline: TRIPOD 2015 Items 16, 17: report calibration plot and calibration test alongside discrimination; AUC alone is insufficient for clinical model evaluation
explanation.trial: Hippisley-Cox et al. QRisk3 (BMJ 2017): cardiovascular risk model — reported both C-statistic (discrimination) and calibration curves by sex and age group — comprehensive model evaluation framework.
explanation.pearl: A model can have high AUC (good at ranking patients) but poor calibration (predicted probabilities are systematically too high or too low). Both must be assessed. Calibration is arguably more important for clinical decision-making — wrong predicted probabilities lead to wrong counselling and wrong treatment thresholds.

questions.stem: Multiple imputation was performed for a surgical database study with 22% missing BMI data. The researcher ran 5 imputations (m=5). A statistician recommends increasing to m=20. What is the primary rationale for this recommendation?

options.A: More imputations reduce the fraction of missing data
options.B: More imputations provide more precise (less variable) estimates of the treatment effect and standard errors, reducing Monte Carlo error from the imputation process
options.C: m=20 imputations are required by regulatory guidelines for all clinical studies
options.D: More imputations always produce different results — m=20 produces the 'correct' answer

explanation.correct: Multiple imputation with m imputations produces m complete datasets, each analysed separately, with results pooled by Rubin's rules. With m=5, there is relatively high Monte Carlo error in the imputed estimates — the pooled coefficients and standard errors have variability due to the small number of imputations. Increasing to m=20 reduces this Monte Carlo variability, producing more stable, reproducible estimates. The rule of thumb for the minimum number of imputations is that m should at least equal the percentage of incomplete cases — with 22% missing data, m=20 is recommended. For high rates of missing data (>50%), even larger numbers of imputations may be needed.

wrong.A: The fraction of missing information (FMI) is determined by the data structure (how much information is in the observed data about the missing values) — it is not reduced by increasing the number of imputations. More imputations reduce Monte Carlo error in estimating what the FMI implies.
wrong.C: There are no universal regulatory requirements for a specific number of imputations. The recommendation is based on methodological guidance (van Buuren, White) related to the fraction of missing information.
wrong.D: MI results converge to a stable estimate as m increases — there is no single 'correct' answer that m=20 achieves uniquely. All m values produce unbiased estimates; larger m simply reduces the imprecision due to finite Monte Carlo sampling.

explanation.guideline: White IR et al. 'Multiple imputation using chained equations: issues and guidance for practice.' Stat Med 2011 — recommended m ≥ fraction missing × 100
explanation.trial: NBOCA analysis (2020 annual report): used m=20 multiple imputation for missing comorbidity data — methodological standard for national audit analysis.
explanation.pearl: A practical rule for the number of imputations: m should be at least equal to the percentage of incomplete cases. For 22% missing data, use m ≥ 22 — m=20 is slightly below but widely accepted. For any missing data analysis, check: what assumptions were made? Were sensitivity analyses under MNAR conducted?

questions.stem: An instrumental variable (IV) analysis is used in an observational study of hospital volume effects on mortality after oesophagectomy. Travel distance to the nearest high-volume centre is used as the instrument. Which property MUST this instrument satisfy to produce valid causal estimates?

options.A: Travel distance must be strongly associated with 90-day mortality (the outcome)
options.B: Travel distance must be strongly associated with being treated at a high-volume centre (the exposure) AND must affect mortality only through its effect on where patients receive surgery (exclusion restriction)
options.C: Travel distance must be randomly assigned by the investigators
options.D: Travel distance must be measured without error in all patients

explanation.correct: A valid instrumental variable must satisfy three conditions: (1) **Relevance**: the instrument must be strongly associated with the exposure (treatment at a high-volume centre) — weak instruments produce biased IV estimates. Tested by the F-statistic in the first-stage regression (F>10 indicates adequate instrument strength). (2) **Independence (exogeneity)**: the instrument must be independent of unmeasured confounders — patients who live further from high-volume centres should not differ systematically in unmeasured ways that affect mortality. (3) **Exclusion restriction**: the instrument affects the outcome ONLY through its effect on the exposure — travel distance affects mortality ONLY by influencing where the patient receives surgery, not through any other pathway (e.g., distance from emergency services post-operatively). The exclusion restriction is not directly testable and requires clinical reasoning.

wrong.A: The instrument must NOT be directly associated with the outcome — it should affect the outcome only through its effect on the exposure (this is the exclusion restriction). Direct association with mortality would violate the exclusion restriction.
wrong.C: Instrumental variables exploit natural variation in the instrument — they are not randomised. The value of IV analysis is precisely that the instrument is not under investigator control.
wrong.D: Measurement error in the instrument reduces its strength (attenuates the first-stage association) but does not invalidate the method in principle — weak instruments with measurement error are a concern, but perfect measurement is not a fundamental assumption.

explanation.guideline: Hernán MA & Robins JM. 'Instruments for causal inference: an epidemiologist's dream?' Epidemiology 2006; Angrist JD & Pischke JS. 'Mostly Harmless Econometrics' — IV conditions
explanation.trial: Birkmeyer JD et al. used distance to high-volume centre as IV for hospital volume in oesophagectomy — found stronger volume-mortality relationship than standard regression, suggesting unmeasured confounding in standard analysis.
explanation.pearl: The exclusion restriction is the most critical and least testable IV assumption. For travel distance IV: does patient distance from a high-volume centre affect mortality through pathways other than which hospital they attend? (Emergency access post-discharge, patient selection by GP referral patterns, etc.) Always discuss exclusion restriction threats explicitly.

questions.stem: A mediation analysis in a surgical trial finds that 60% of the effect of laparoscopic vs open colorectal surgery on 30-day complications is mediated through reduced surgical site infection. The remaining 40% is a direct effect. What is the correct interpretation?

options.A: 60% of patients in the laparoscopic group had no surgical site infection
options.B: The reduction in complications associated with laparoscopic surgery occurs partly through reduction of surgical site infection (indirect effect, 60%) and partly through other mechanisms not mediated by SSI (direct effect, 40%)
options.C: The laparoscopic approach directly causes 60% fewer complications than open surgery
options.D: The mediation analysis proves that SSI causes 60% of all complications after colorectal surgery

explanation.correct: Mediation analysis decomposes the total effect of an exposure (laparoscopic surgery) on an outcome (complications) into: (1) the **indirect effect** — the path through the mediator (complications reduced because laparoscopic surgery reduces SSI, which reduces overall complication rate); and (2) the **direct effect** — the residual effect of laparoscopic surgery on complications through all mechanisms NOT involving SSI (e.g., reduced anastomotic leak, reduced pneumonia). A 60% indirect effect and 40% direct effect means that SSI reduction accounts for 60% of the total complication benefit of laparoscopic surgery. This has clinical implications: targeted SSI prevention could capture 60% of the benefit even in open surgery.

wrong.A: 60% indirect effect refers to the proportion of the treatment effect mediated, not to the proportion of patients with or without SSI.
wrong.C: 60% here is the proportion of the effect mediated (not the magnitude of effect reduction). The actual percentage reduction in complications is a different statistic.
wrong.D: Mediation analysis identifies causal pathways between the exposure and outcome — it does not establish general aetiological relationships between SSI and all complications independent of the treatment context.

explanation.guideline: VanderWeele TJ. 'Explanation in Causal Inference: Methods for Mediation and Interaction.' Oxford University Press 2015 — definitive text on mediation analysis in epidemiology
explanation.trial: Blumetti J et al. (2010): mediation analysis showing that SSI prevention strategies mediate a substantial proportion of length-of-stay reduction — same conceptual framework applied to a quality improvement question.
explanation.pearl: Mediation analysis requires strong causal assumptions: no unmeasured confounding of the exposure-outcome, exposure-mediator, or mediator-outcome relationships. In observational surgical data, these assumptions are rarely fully met — results should be interpreted cautiously as suggestive of mechanisms rather than definitive causal estimates.

questions.stem: In a surgical randomised trial, the statistician reports a statistically significant treatment × time interaction (p=0.01) in a mixed effects model of pain scores over 4 post-operative time points. What does this interaction signify?

options.A: The treatment effect on pain is constant across all time points
options.B: The pain trajectories over time differ significantly between the treatment groups — the benefit of the treatment increases, decreases, or changes in direction at different time points
options.C: The interaction indicates a serious model mis-specification error that invalidates the analysis
options.D: A significant interaction confirms that the treatment is effective at all time points

explanation.correct: A treatment × time interaction in a mixed effects model means that the difference in pain scores between groups changes across time — the treatment effect is not constant. Possible patterns: (1) the treatment group shows faster pain recovery (converging curves — early benefit that narrows over time); (2) delayed effect (curves diverge over time — benefit increases); (3) crossing effect (treatment group initially worse but eventually better). The interaction p=0.01 indicates this time-varying treatment effect is statistically significant. Clinical interpretation requires examining the estimated marginal means at each time point, not just the overall treatment effect.

wrong.A: A constant treatment effect across time would produce a non-significant interaction (p>0.05) — the significant interaction specifically indicates the treatment effect VARIES across time.
wrong.C: A treatment × time interaction is a pre-specified inferential target in longitudinal models — it is an informative result, not a model error.
wrong.D: A significant interaction means the treatment effect differs across time — it does not confirm benefit at all time points. The treatment might be beneficial at early time points but equivalent at later time points (or vice versa).

explanation.guideline: Molenberghs G & Verbeke G. 'Models for Discrete Longitudinal Data.' Springer 2005; ICH E9 — longitudinal outcomes require pre-specification of whether the primary test is the overall treatment effect or the time × treatment interaction
explanation.trial: RECOVERY-RS trial (respiratory support for COVID-19): mixed model with treatment × time interaction for SpO2 trajectory — interaction p=0.02 revealed diverging benefit over time for one respiratory support modality.
explanation.pearl: When reporting a treatment × time interaction, show estimated marginal means (with 95% CIs) at each time point plotted graphically — this communicates the clinical trajectory far more effectively than the interaction coefficient alone.

questions.stem: A LASSO (Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator) regularisation approach is used instead of stepwise regression for developing a prediction model of pancreatic fistula after distal pancreatectomy with 200 events and 40 candidate variables. What is the PRIMARY advantage of LASSO over stepwise regression?

options.A: LASSO always selects exactly the same variables regardless of the dataset used
options.B: LASSO applies a shrinkage penalty that reduces overfitting, handles correlated predictors better than stepwise, and selects variables simultaneously rather than sequentially — producing more calibrated predictions in external validation
options.C: LASSO is computationally faster and therefore preferred for large datasets
options.D: LASSO provides unbiased coefficient estimates, while stepwise regression estimates are biased

explanation.correct: LASSO regression penalises the sum of absolute values of coefficients (L1 penalty), shrinking less important coefficients to exactly zero (automatic variable selection) while retaining important predictors. Advantages over stepwise regression: (1) simultaneous variable selection — not sequential, so no path dependency; (2) built-in shrinkage — coefficients are penalised toward zero, reducing overfitting even for retained predictors; (3) handles correlated predictors better — stepwise is unstable with correlated variables, arbitrarily selecting one; (4) better external validation performance because shrinkage reduces optimism. The penalty parameter λ is optimised by cross-validation. For prediction modelling, LASSO with cross-validated λ is now the methodological standard.

wrong.A: LASSO variable selection does depend on the specific dataset — with different random samples from the same population, LASSO may select slightly different variable sets. Stability of LASSO selection should be assessed by bootstrap.
wrong.C: Computational efficiency is not the primary reason to prefer LASSO — modern stepwise regression is also computationally fast. The methodological advantages (shrinkage, simultaneous selection) are the primary justification.
wrong.D: LASSO coefficients are biased by the shrinkage penalty — they are biased toward zero. This bias is intentional and beneficial (reduces variance and overfitting), but LASSO does not provide unbiased estimates. Post-LASSO inference (using LASSO for selection then OLS for estimation) can recover less biased estimates.

explanation.guideline: Tibshirani R. 'Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso.' J R Stat Soc B 1996 — original LASSO paper; Harrell FE 'Regression Modeling Strategies' — recommends LASSO over stepwise for all prediction modelling tasks
explanation.trial: Andreou A et al. (Br J Surg 2018): LASSO-selected prediction model for pancreatic fistula outperformed stepwise model in external validation — demonstrated real-world superiority of regularisation approach.
explanation.pearl: The 10 events-per-variable (EPV) rule for logistic regression recommends no more than k = events/10 variables. With 200 events and 40 candidates, EPV = 5 — this violates the EPV rule and makes LASSO (which handles high dimensionality) the appropriate approach over unrestricted maximum likelihood estimation.
